data.title: Dick's Sporting Goods: Quick Bounce Returns Shares To Fair Value
data.description:
There's a lot to like with Dick's Sporting Goods (NYSE:DKS). The company seems incredibly well-run. DKS stock has provided total returns of 260% since Lauren Hobart was named chief executive officer in February 2021; Hobart's well-respected predecessor, Ed Stack, remains chairman and heavily involved with the company.

Financial performance has been impressive: based on guidance, adjusted earnings per share in fiscal 2025 (ending January 2026) will rise nearly fourfold from its level six years earlier. And even in a seemingly tough macroeconomic environment, Dick's is driving same-store sales growth, with a 5% print in Q2 and a full-year outlook of 2.0 to 3.5%.

There certainly are reasons to own Dick's stock, particularly with a ~5% sell-off after earnings on Thursday morning. And I did own the stock, taking a position after the market's irrational sell-off following the announcement that Dick's would acquire Foot Locker (FL). But I also exited before this week's release — and don't see quite enough reason to jump back in just yet.

Is Foot Locker Accretive?

The rally in DKS to pre-merger levels has modestly changed the calculus of the deal. After the initial sell-off — in which Dick's lost $2.5 billion in market cap on an acquisition that cost $2.5 billion — it seemed like Dick's would be paying for the deal mostly in cash. Foot Locker shareholders would receive either $24 per share in cash or 0.1168 shares of Dick's stock; choosing the latter would require a DKS stock price of $205.48. DKS closed the day of the announcement barely above $175, but with the deadline today, FL shareholders are going to choose the stock option.

That shift from cash to stock doesn't change the numbers much; the assumption of Foot Locker's post-Q2 net debt still leaves the overall cost at the previously announced $2.5 billion. But it does change the math around earnings going forward, with Dick's now seeing an inflated share count versus increased net interest expense. Two different analysts on the second quarter conference call asked whether the acquisition still would be accretive to FY26 earnings per share. Chief financial officer Navdeep Gupta responded to one questioner that "we continue to remain confident that this [transaction] would be accretive," but hedged a bit during his answer.

The midpoint of FY25 guidance for Dick's is adjusted net income of $1.15 billion (81 million shares at $14.20 per share). Foot Locker hasn't given guidance since Q4 owing to the sale announcement, but it seems clear from first-half results that its original range of $1.35-$1.65 per share was far too optimistic. Right now, the consensus estimate is 77 cents per share, and it's not clear how fresh the underlying projections are.

Giving Foot Locker credit for $75 million in net income (using that consensus estimate), Dick's total adjusted net income gets to $1.225 billion. An projected $100-$125 million in synergies don't likely get achieved in year one, but even assuming they do that (after-tax) adds ~$85 million, putting net income at $1.31 billion. The updated share count of ~92 million suggests current pro forma adjusted EPS of $14.24 — pennies above the current midpoint for Dick's as a standalone.

In other words, right now, the math suggests that Foot Locker is unlikely to be accretive next year (again, the synergies are not going to hit the P&L instantly). Gupta in fact said on the merger call that the synergies would be realized "in the medium term". And what's notable here is that unless Foot Locker grows from FY25, even pro forma for synergies the deal will be a multi-year drag on earnings.

Betting On A Foot Locker Turnaround

To be fair, that doesn't mean the deal is already a bust. The point of the acquisition was not necessarily to own a growing business; Foot Locker's challenges were myriad, long-running and well-known. Hobart and her team clearly believe they can turn the business around, and there are other advantages such as increased negotiating power with Nike (NKE). (As noted last time, the combined company should account for a high-teens percentage of the shoe giant's overall sales.)

But the math does highlight two key points here. The first is that FY26 estimates are likely coming down. Right now, the Street projects ~7% growth to $15.30 per share, but that is a big reach if Foot Locker is still treading water at best. As good as Dick's Q2 print looks from a headline perspective, the guidance hike was relatively small (a lower projected share count helped, though a higher expected tax rate offset that) and still only suggests about 1% growth from FY24. I'm loath to write off analyst expectations based on a single data point (those analysts are obviously doing the work), but expecting double-digit net income growth from Dick's or a rebound in Foot Locker seems overly optimistic here.

The second point is that, over the next 12-18 months, the market is probably going to have look through the Foot Locker drag to see the underlying business. That's probably likely to happen, but only if the market truly trusts Hobart's vision for Foot Locker and sees some sign of progress there. Hobart has probably earned that trust, but after Foot Locker's Q2 (in which the company missed earnings estimates badly and saw same-store sales decline), the soon-to-be-acquired business clearly has not. That doesn't necessarily mean a double-digit sell-off (Foot Locker accounts for ~12% of the current enterprise value for Dick's) but it does get to the idea that investors really need to trust management to execute on the merger and deliver on their vision for Foot Locker.

Is Dick's Peaking?

Obviously, the same is true for Dick's itself. It certainly seems like management is doing the job well at the moment. Same-store sales in the first half grew 4.7%. Guidance does suggest a deceleration to the ~1% range in the second half, but as Gupta noted on the call Q4 FY24 comps were +6.4%, creating a difficult comparison for the holiday season.

And relative to expectations for FY26 and beyond, there is some room for upside. The GameChanger app for youth sports is adding ~$60 million in revenue this year (it's growing 50% off a base of over $100 million last year) and that should continue. Investments behind the business hit operating margins by ~100 basis points in Q2; those may not repeat, providing a mid- to high-single-digit to profits next year. Pre-opening costs for new House of Sports and Field House locations are not insignificant (and are not excluded from the company's non-GAAP figures); guidance suggests an after-tax hit this year of about 65 cents per share.

So off the $14.20 base in FY25 EPS, there is a path toward a model perhaps in the high teens over the mid-term, simply from the legacy business. GameChanger should be adding 20 cents-plus per share; reduced investment intensity perhaps another 50 cents or more, with continued organic growth (including market share gains and further expansion of the newer concepts) doing the rest. And if Hobart and Stack are correct, or close, on Foot Locker, the multi-year growth outlook gets even stronger.

Obviously, that path depends on Hobart and Gupta pulling the right levers, whether it's in negotiations with Nike, managing tariffs, or executing the expansion of House of Sport and Field House. And, again, at this point investors probably are relatively to keep betting on a team that has provided such stellar returns. (Those returns aren't new, either: since the 2002 initial public offering, DKS stock has posted annualized total returns of about 22%.)

But there are two core concerns here. The first, of course, is the macroeconomic environment. Management was notably upbeat about that environment on the Q2 call, with Hobart saying the company was "not seeing any signs of slowdown with the consumer". For a cyclical retailer, however, that's not necessarily a good thing: investors presumably are paying for what now look like profits that are near, if perhaps not quite at, the peak.

And that leads to the second concern, which is valuation. Even a generous read of current earnings power, adding back pre-opening costs and assuming that some of this year's investments in infrastructure and marketing don't repeat, suggests non-GAAP earnings per share maybe getting to $16. That's a multiple of about 13.4x at the current DKS stock price, right in the company's multi-year range:

To be fair, an investor can see that multiple as quite a good thing. Paying a multiple in the historical range has been a winning investment.

But the flip side is that paying a multiple within the historical range can be dangerous near the top of a cycle. It's possible this isn't a cyclical issue, and that Dick's is benefiting from the broader expansion/explosion of spending on youth sports, in particular, but it's not difficult to imagine the macro environment (and tariffs) eventually becoming a problem in the not-too-distant future. That's a risk here, even with DKS stock seemingly reasonably valued.

At $175 following the May sell-off, that risk simply wasn't as large: DKS was at about 13x this year's earnings, and closer to a low double-digit multiple assuming any net contribution from the Foot Locker deal. That was particularly true since it seemed at least possible that the market would revise its initial reaction to the Foot Locker acquisition relatively quickly. (That, indeed, was what happened.) At $214 now, the risk/reward has changed; DKS looks much more fairly valued, and much more of a 'Hold' than a 'Buy'.

An Exception To The Retail Rule?

It's possible my personal view here is skewed somewhat by a long-running skepticism toward retailers. This simply has been a hugely difficult industry, albeit one in which Dick's clearly has been an exception. But success in the space generally has required trying to find a clearly discounted price (as happened with the post-announcement sell-off) and keeping an eye out for potential reversals in broader trends. (Among them might be a return of a more promotional environment, something mentioned in passing on the Q2 call.)

And there is a cautionary tale here. It's not hard to see echoes of the Dick's story in that of Best Buy (BBY) in the late 2010s. There, too, a new CEO (Herbert Joly) drove quick results and early optimism. There, too, market share consolidation was a big factor (though Dick's doesn't have quite the same dominance). And for Best Buy, the trend of cheaper, better electronics throughout the decade, as well as better-than-expected competition against Amazon (AMZN) in particular, led to impressive returns.

But that story eventually stalled out; the gravity of U.S. retail with its thin margins, wage pressures, and intense, price-driven competition eventually caught up. So far this decade, BBY stock has returned 2.5% — total.

To be sure, I don't expect those kind of returns for DKS going forward. There is a lot to like, and the potential for the newer initiatives to drive growth if the legacy business simply stays stable. But there are real risks here, and a valuation that requires Dick's essentially keep doing what it has been able to do for the last few years. In a difficult industry and likely a difficult environment, that's far from guaranteed.

data.title: BlackRock Equity Dividend Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The BlackRock Equity Dividend Fund is a conservative, core holding with a long-term track record for consistently capturing dividend growth through diverse market conditions. Dividend-paying stocks are an attractive source of income in today’s market environment.

Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 5.60% (Institutional shares) and 5.52% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were security selection in the health care sector and investment decisions in the information technology (IT) sector. The largest detractor was stock selection in the industrials and utilities sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the financials, health care, and industrials sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the communication services and IT sectors, and reduced its exposures to the health care and industrials sectors.
Contributors Detractors
In the health care sector, stock selection in the health care providers& services industry was beneficial. Investment decisions in the IT sector contributed, particularly in the software industry. Lastly, selection decisions in the consumer staples sector, specifically in the consumer staples distribution & retail industry, had a positive impact. The largest detractor was investment decisions in the industrials sector, most notably an underweight position in the electrical equipment industry. Stock selection in the utilities sector negatively impacted relative performance, particularly in the electric utilities industry.

Further insight

At quarter-end, markets remain volatile, shaped by trade and geopolitical tensions, and mixed economic signals. However, optimism about artificial intelligence remains, with companies emphasizing the technology in earnings calls and delivering strong results. We remain anchored in our bottom-up, fundamentals-driven approach. While corporate earnings are healthy, full valuations demand precision and discipline. In this environment, our value-oriented strategy, which is focused on companies with strong balance sheets, durable cash flows, and reasonable valuations, offers stability. We believe this approach provides critical support, helping to navigate uncertainty and capitalize on opportunities through active management.

data.title: Victoria's Secret Is Challenged In The U.S., Stock Price Is More Reasonable
data.description:
Victoria's Secret & Co. (NYSE:VSCO) reported relatively muted Q2 and H1 '25 results.

The company is not growing much in the aggregate, and I have reasons to believe that the U.S. business is doing significantly worse than last year. Margins were ok, but the company is expecting a less profitable H2 '25, given the impact of tariffs.

Since I last wrote about VSCO, with a Hold rating "due to the high expectations already priced in and the potential for rapid sentiment shifts," the stock is down 53%. At current prices (~10/13x guided FY25 earnings), it seems much more reasonable, albeit I remain worried about execution, and maintain a Hold rating.

Q2 '25 results

Last time I wrote about VSCO (after the Q3 '24 report, in late 2024), the company seemed to be stepping out of a cyclical downturn, with comps improving 3%. However, the stock price had become so optimistic that a recovery was not enough, with a complete growth rally already priced. As of H1 '25, the growth rally has not occurred, margins have deteriorated, and the stock has puffed.

Meager growth on easier comps: The company posted comps up 3% in Q2 '25 and 1% in H1 '25. Although this is a regular result, we have to consider that it comes from -3% and -4% respectively in H1 '24, meaning the results are still not above some pretty bad comps from H1 '24. Compared to 2024, the Google Trends are about flat as of late August.

Further, we are building a four-year track record of down comp sales, with FY24 flat versus FY23, FY23 down 9% versus FY22, and FY22 down 8% versus FY21. This is important when considering the potential for turnaround and execution.

International shows value of brand: The U.S. is the main driver of the company's revenues (55% from stores, up to 85% when considering DTC), but I'm not so sure about operating income.

The company does not disclose segment operational income, only revenues, but reading the 10-K, one can notice that the international revenues are mostly franchise revenues and that

revenue recognized under franchise and license arrangements generally consists of royalties earned and recognized upon the sale of merchandise by franchise and license partners to retail customers.

The above means that international revenues are probably almost pure gross margin, because royalties have very little cost.

The good point is that the company is doing very well internationally, with sales increasing 22% for the quarter, and partner-operated stores expected to increase 10% for the year.

Another sign of brand value is the fact that the beauty category (the largest part of partner-managed fleet abroad, and generally licensed) is in its 8th quarter of growth.

Tariff challenges: Despite growth in the most profitable business (by margins, not necessarily in the aggregate), the company's gross margins were flat (+20bps), and are expected to contract in Q3. The company said that this was caused by better full price selling (positive for margins), offset by higher freight and tariffs.

Because of international growth, I would have expected the general gross margins to move higher. The fact that they didn't leads me to believe that U.S. gross margins are actually much more challenged.

SG&A deleverage: On the same vein, if the sales that are almost pure profit (royalties) are growing so much, then it is hard not to leverage SG&A and operating margins. However, the company actually delevered operating margins by 60bps both for the quarter and first half.

What this indicates, again, is that probably operating margins in the U.S. business are doing much worse than on the aggregate, which is lifted by International, but still down.

Lower profitability expected for the year

The company is expecting to have more challenges in H2 '25. It will face worse comps, as H2 '24 was one of growth. It will also face more challenges from tariff impacts on CoGS (or in pricing).

The company expects adjusted operating income of $270/320 million versus $375 million last year, and adjusted FCF of $150/200 million versus $286 million last year. FCF is expected to almost halve versus last year.

Potential interested party

In late May, the company announced that it had adopted a rights program in order to prevent one shareholder from gaining a larger stake in the company. The shareholder has accumulated a mid-teens stake and has stopped buying after the rights offering.

We have seen significant acquisition activity in the consumer discretionary space. The ones that come to mind more easily are Guess? (GES) and Skechers (SKX).

The ones not buying shares are the company's insiders, showing no new money share buys in the last twelve months. All "insider accumulation" has come from share-based payments. Indeed, shareholders have been diluted so far this year.

Valuation is fair but not super attractive

The company expects EPS for the year of $1.8/2.2, which compares to a share price of $22 as a multiple of 10/12x. On an FCF basis, the FCF yield guided for the year is 10% at the midpoint ($180 million versus market cap of $1.8 billion).

These are not high valuation multiples, especially considering the value of the brand. The company is growing outside the U.S., because Victoria's Secret still commands a value in many regions.

In addition, we have two potential tailwinds.

First, the interested party above, which could potentially make an offer for the company, or part of it.

Second, the removal of the de-minimis tariff exemption in the U.S., helping all large apparel companies versus their competitors from China, like Shein and Temu. I do not know how much of the company's market was affected by Shein/Temu competition (given that quality is probably a bigger concern in categories like Beauty and Innerwear). Still, this could prove positive.

We also have two headwinds.

The first is the disposable income impact of tariffs, which could affect sales in the U.S. Related to this, the impact of higher tariff costs on company margins and productivity. This is already evident in H1 '25, if one accounts for the offsetting positive effect from International growth.

The second is the company's management, which has repeatedly failed at execution since the company went public. I have covered the different failures in more detail in previous articles. The company has recently replaced its CEO and CFO, but I'm still uncertain about management and Board quality.

Overall, I think the price is fairish, considering the pros and cons. However, I do not think it is super attractive, because I do not see a path to growth given the challenges in the U.S. and the lack of execution.

For the time being, I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Who's In Charge Of The U.S. Economy (If Not The Fed)?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Lyn Alden Schwartzer, founder of Lyn Alden Investment Strategy and contributing analyst to ElliottWaveTrader.net, discusses fiscal dominance - the rising influence of government deficits and spending over central banks on markets and inflation. She explains how this shift creates a hotter, less cyclical economic environment and what it means for investors navigating these changes in 2025.

Transcript

Mike Larson: In the world of fiscal dominance, the rules are different. Economies and markets behave differently. And if you don't change your approach as an investor, it can cost you dearly. That's why I was so pleased to speak to this week's guest. Her top-down macroeconomic analysis is superb, and her comprehensive knowledge of bank lending drivers, technological developments, digital assets, and the intersection of policy and markets is second to none. Her name is Lyn Alden Schwartzer, and she is founder of Lyn Alden Investment Strategy and an analyst at elliottwavetrader.net. Almost 800,000 follow Lyn's work on X. More than 322,000 viewers have watched her recent presentation at Bitcoin 2025 on YouTube. And now Lyn is joining me here on the MoneyShow MoneyMasters podcast.

Lyn, thank you so much for joining me here today. I mean, there's so much to talk about on the macro front. I know a lot of your work is kind of based on that macro overlay. But just this is the first time I've had you on the podcast - obviously, I follow your work, but some of our viewers may not have - why don't you talk a little bit about your background and what it is you do, at this point, then we'll kick off from there.

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Sure. So thanks for having me. My initial background was actually Electrical Engineering, and I then went into System Engineering, Engineering Management. But I've had a lifelong interest in investing. I've been doing that alongside engineering for a long time, and eventually, I left engineering to focus on analysis full time. I take some of a novel approach of using actually kind of systems analysis, in my market analysis. So, basically, when I look at, capital flows and things like that, I treat it like I would a system, with inputs, outputs, governing logic, because these are, at the end of the day, human-designed systems. Obviously, it's very, very complex ones, with a lot of moving pieces. And so, at lynalden.com, I cover equities, commodities, other asset classes, and I'm the author of Broken Money. Came out in 2023, and it catalogs the history of kind of the intersection of technology and money.

Mike Larson: Great. Well, I appreciate that background. My father being a Mechanical Engineer for many years in aerospace at Pratt, when -- he's going to enjoy this segment, I'm sure. Let's talk about one of the big points that you had earlier this year, and that's fiscal dominance. What does that concept mean? What does it mean for markets in the economy? And I know that that was a lot of what you talked about at the beginning of the year. How have things changed or not changed now that we're talking in late August of 2025?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So for the most part, they've not changed. Kind of the whole point of fiscal dominance is, it tends to last for quite a while. And so, basically, what that means is that unlike the past 40-plus years, where monetary dominance was in play, basically the bank lending decisions and by extension, the central bank, tailoring its policy to either encourage more lending or pull back lending, that was monetary dominance. Now we're in more of a fiscal dominance environment, meaning that the size and the direction of the deficits are, generally speaking, larger variable than the central bank and even kind of the broader banking system. Really, the only thing that's changed since then is, obviously, tariffs have heated up recently, and so that's a slight break on the fiscal deficits because you're bringing in more revenue, so far mostly from the American public. And so that kind of offsets the deficits, but that's still fairly small in the grand scheme of things.

We are still running above baseline deficits as a percentage of GDP. In addition, we have more information from the Fed, which is that it looks like they're going to be a little bit more dovish going forward. So while variables have changed, we're still largely in play in terms of fiscal dominance.

Mike Larson: Sure. And I definitely want to talk about the Fed a little bit and what we've just learned in the past week on that front. But let's talk about a fiscally dominated market/economy versus one where monetary policy is the main force. What does that mean for an investor? What do they sort of need to know about those two dynamics?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So on one hand, it tends to run hotter, which is to say that, when you're running a larger deficit, all else being equal, that's somewhat of a stimulus, with the cost often being basically debasement or inflation, at least above target otherwise would be. And it also tends to be less cyclical because a lot of those deficits are not really tied to the business cycle per se. If anything, they increase during a recession. And so while it's still possible to have recession and you still do have cycles, they tend to be somewhat muted. It's kind of like how -- if you look at equities, there are obviously certain equity sectors that are a lot more cyclical than others, whereas some like defense or health care just tend to be on their own cycles compared to these more business cycle ones. And what happens in fiscal dominance is kind of the broader economy tends to look more like those types of industries to some extent because the deficits are kind of always on compared to the more cyclical nature of bank lending.

One way to kind of put it is that over the past 70 plus years, for the majority of the time, net new annual bank loans, and you can even add corporate bonds, so bank loans plus net new bonds issued, that was a larger sum than public deficits. It only kind of in brief recessions do you see that the public deficit would be greater than that kind of private sector lending. Whereas now, we generally see even outside of recessions in recent years, the public deficit is larger than the sum of all new bank loans and corporate bond issuance in a typical year. And so that just -- it's not to say that there's other things that aren't relevant, but they're a smaller share of the pie than they used to be.

Mike Larson: And is this something we're seeing globally, not just in the U.S.? I mean, obviously, China's been sort of on this. You know, we got to do more stimulus. We got to do more stimulus-type approach. Some of the things the administration's been doing seem to be getting places like Europe rethinking the deficit spending. What are your thoughts on that?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So it's more so a U.S. phenomenon than a global phenomenon, but on a country-by-country basis, it's certainly relevant. I think there's a cycle that countries tend to go through. The U.S. currently is running larger deficits as a share of GDP than most European nations. It's part of the structure of the euro itself that makes that a little bit more difficult to run larger sustained deficits. But they all have kind of similar dynamics, which is older demographics. They fuel some of the deficit spending because you have that more top-heavy entitlement system. So whether it's China, whether it's Europe, whether it's US, they all kind of are in that boat together.

And it just so happens that because U.S. health care is very expensive and the way we kind of structured our entitlement spending, as well as, generally speaking, a bigger share on defense as well, we're running hotter than the rest of those. But things matter around the margins. So the fact that Europe's running hotter than is normal for Europe is still relevant from an investing standpoint. It's just an absolute sense the U.S. is really kind of fiscally the most dominant.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, let's shift gears here a little bit. You brought up the Fed, and obviously, we had Jay Powell essentially. I mean, not guaranteed, but strongly suggest the Fed's going to be cutting when September rolls around. There's certainly been plenty of political pressure on the Fed to operate and do more than that. What are your thoughts on what Powell said, what it means, and sort of what the path is forward for the rest of '25 and as we roll into '26?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Yeah. I think he took a slightly dovish take compared to what the market expected, and the market kind of pretty much priced that in as soon as he was speaking about it. And basically, what we should expect is generally at least slightly lower interest rates. He made no commitment to the idea that he's going to keep doing it in 2026, but he did heavily imply that we're at least getting one or two cuts perhaps, upcoming.

One of the challenges - this goes back to fiscal dominance - is their industry tool is mostly around trying to get banks to either lend more or lend less because, historically speaking, during periods where inflation's hotter, there tends to be a lot of money creation from bank lending. So for example, in the '70s, when the baby boomers were entering their home buying years, that was kind of the peak period of U.S. loan formation. There's a lot of loans being done and greater than average money supply growth and the inflation that they came with. Obviously, there are other factors like the oil shortages and things like that where that money supply growth actually does translate into much higher prices because you don't necessarily have that productivity offset. But basically, that bank lending is a really big component of it. And so most of the tools are around that.

The problem is that in this whole kind of COVID and post-COVID environment, because we're in fiscal dominance, the inflation we've seen has not been from excess bank lending. Bank lending rates are pretty muted. If anything, they're kind of historically normal and on the lower side. And so the Fed trying to keep rates high to slow down bank lending is kind of working around the problem. The actual core of the -- what fueled all this was the deficits. Back in -- years ago, it was a stimulus, and now it's a less extreme but more sustained, just ongoing fiscal spending, which tends to run things hot.

And so the Fed kind of find themselves in a position where there's no right answer. If they cut rates, it doesn't look great because they're cutting rates into, like all-time high stocks, gold, bitcoin, low employment. It looks -- it kind of looks dovish on that surface.

On the other hand, the housing market is pretty much at a standstill. I think even with some cuts, it's not really going to change that, and bank lending is not the issue. So I can certainly see why they'd make a case for cutting. And it's just -- the fiscal dominance is more about the fiscal entity than what the central bank's going into.

Mike Larson: Got it. You brought up markets, so I think that's a logical pivot here. I mean, obviously, we've seen precious metals be extremely strong. We've seen bitcoin, as you mentioned, do very well and certainly other cryptos, in the digital asset space as well. What are your thoughts on what's primarily driving it? Is it all the I need safety, purchasing power protection? Is it a lot of the projects going on in the digital space? I mean, how much some -- in terms of driving factors of those kinds of asset classes are similar or versus different in your opinion?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So in the digital asset space, I think basically right now, corporations are the bulk of what's driving it. So if you look at, kind of where sales have come from, it's generally come from those who've held for a while and are up pretty significantly on their gains, and then they either -- they sell to rebalance or to increase their consumption level in some way. Basically, for a lot of people, 100,000 bitcoin was kind of their magic number. You know, some of them have been holding since a 1000 a coin or even less. Some people have been holding since 10,000, but you kind of hit that psychologically important number. You get this ongoing kind of distributive effect.

Another hand, there's -- Strategy has been purchasing. Metaplanet has been purchasing. And then we've seen this kind of breakout in other -- like altcoin treasury companies. I think, like kind of prior cycles, it probably won't end well. A lot of companies filled up with kind of tertiary altcoins. It's probably not something we look back on years from now and say, "yeah that was a good thing to do." But, so I think it's -- that's kind of where that rotation's happening away from kind of older whales and more toward these kind of corporate treasuries until they get satiated for this particular cycle.

Mike Larson: Okay. And while we're on that subject, I mean, obviously, we've seen bitcoin in particular, but some of the other coins get more mainstream money coming in as a result of changing policies on the sort of advisor side, plus the rollout of all these ETFs that make it easy for people who are non-crypto people to invest. Where do you think we are in that trend? I mean, is this still early stages, mid stages? What are your thoughts on that?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: I still think it's pretty early stages. I think that things can get ahead of themselves for periods of time. So for example, when the ETFs came out, there was kind of like a local bubble, and bitcoin kind of end up chopping sideways the next seven months because of it as things had to kind of work itself out. And then, with the election and the rapid change in perception around what the regulation environment would be, that also gave us another really big pop, in bitcoin, which took another kind of seven months or so to absorb before we went on to new highs again.

So while things can get ahead of themselves temporarily, I still think that in the zooming-out sense, they were still pretty early. Most companies don't have bitcoin on their balance sheet, and I think even in the next five years, that'll still be the case. That'll still be a niche thing. Most governments still don't have it on their balance sheet. A handful do in sovereign wealth funds and things like that. And most pensions, they might have a little slice here or there. But I just think that in the grand scheme of how much capital's out there, bitcoin as a $2 trillion asset is still pretty small in the grand scheme of things. Even though basically it's this global money that's still smaller than some individual equities are in terms of market cap. And so I still think it's pretty early in the kind of integration of the digital asset space and then broader traditional markets.

Mike Larson: Got it. I do want to talk about kind of your market outlook. But before I do, obviously, given your engineering background and some of what you said recently on X, you talked about how you use AI for -- in your own business, but there are certain things like summarizing novels and high-priority art projects that it's still not maybe -- the reality hasn't caught up to the hype. I'm just kind of curious as to your perspective, again, as a business person as well as just sort of the fascination with that part of the market, what your thoughts are?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: I mean, directionally, I think we're going similar to full self-driving, which is some of the proponents would always say it's like two years away, and it's been like 10 years of that. And, of course, in some areas, it can work in pretty kind of controlled areas. But in the grand scheme of things, anything in the -- problem is anything in the field is way, way harder. It's always easy to automate things in controlled environments than to automate things where there's always edge cases everywhere. And so, full self-driving has kind of eluded the technical optimists.

And, I was kind of writing that in response to basically seeing people say, in five years, like, it'll displace all of our jobs and things like that. While obviously, there are certain areas that it's very -- it's already eating into. It's pretty ripe to do that because it kind of is those like controlled environments, where it can -- it can kind of iterate, learn, and then just do things with minimal edge cases to worry about.

Anytime you get into the field, so when we talk about like robotics in the field, for example, it's got basically that full self-driving problem on steroids because even the roads themselves are somewhat of a controlled environment, and yet it's still hard, so let alone everything else that might have to be done. So while I think that AI will keep progressing, I think it's already -- it's good enough that it can impact more than it already has. It just takes time to kind of integrate into business and consumer processes.

I generally fade the really kind of hyping super optimistic view or some people view it as a negative thing, but basically, the technical optimist view that these things are just going to be so dramatically impactful compared to humans in five years, I think is -- I think that's too optimistic. I think we have to have more sober outlooks on it.

Mike Larson: Fair enough. When it comes to markets and asset classes, I mean, obviously, equities have had a huge rally. We're close to or at highs depending which index you're looking at. Gold is still sort of kicking around that 3,400 level and bitcoin pulling back between a 110, 115, give or take. Really speaking about those asset classes and maybe if you want to throw the dollar out there too, what are you generally still bullish on? What are you getting more cautious on? And where do you think there might be a reversal out there that we're not speaking about?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So I'm still bullish on bitcoin. Gold, I started worrying about this spring because it got kind of heated. Fortunately, that's been more of a sideways consolidation than a correction. And I think it's kind of burning off some of that heat kind of like bitcoin had to do a couple times this cycle. So I'm still long-term bullish on it, but I -- it's still kind of working through that.

I'm increasingly a little cautious on U.S. equities at these levels, given the ongoing headwinds that are still there, including tariffs and things like that. I also think that rate cuts, while helpful, are probably not going to be as stimulative as people think because without a kind of lower low in rate, especially longer-term rates, I think any sort of like home refinancing cycle is going to be pretty muted this time compared to prior cycles. And so while it helps certain entities and individuals around the margins, I don't think that we'll have any sort of big gains from that rate-cutting cycle.

Where I tend to be more bullish is foreign equities. I still think they're very under-owned. In many cases, it's still fairly inexpensive, and upcoming dovish cycle should be somewhat beneficial to them. So, I tend to think there's a lot of -- kind of returns to be had over the next three to five years for more of that foreign sector.

Mike Larson: Okay. And again, everybody has sort of different policies about this. Any particular stocks, ETFs, sector ETFs that you think are more promising than others that you'd be comfortable naming, or you just want to kind of keep it at that macro level?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: I would say at the macro level, although I would point out that I tend to be interested in some of the financials in those areas. So I've been interested in Latin American financials, for example, basically, things on the more value side of that. And these kind of -- when people talk about like equity rotations, it's always hard. There's been this very like a long line of people calling for rotation over the past 5, 10 years. We've had just kind of, pretty miraculous outperformance of U.S. equities compared to the foreign market.

I think the kind of key thing to keep in mind is, anytime there's a rate cutting cycle, that's kind of like the shields down, where if rotations are going to happen, that's a really good catalyst for it to happen. It ended up not happening during the kind of the pre-COVID rate cutting cycle because we had all these other factors coming in at once. So it's not to say that a cutting cycle does cause a rotation, but when you already have, basically, all this global capital on one side of the boat stuffed into U.S. markets, huge period of outperformance, and then you do get ongoing cutting cycles, that tends to be a useful catalyst.

And really, since the Fed started cutting last year, at least a little bit, we have generally seen an uptick in performance of international markets. In many kind of areas, they've kind of at least stopped underperforming. They haven't had some sort of major positive rotation yet, but at least there is kind of a bottoming if you look at some of these ratio charts. And I think it's useful to keep an eye to see if that sustains. I expect that it will, but I'm kind of fully aware that this has been one of the hardest things to call as any sort of change in leadership.

Mike Larson: Okay. I guess probably the last asset class I haven't talked all that much about would be what to do in the fixed income space. I mean, do you see things in terms of credit quality that concern you? Do you have any thoughts on the yield curve and so on? People are looking to trade those types of trends. Where do you stand on them?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: So for, like, five years, I've been a pretty outspoken bond bear. But now that yields are higher, I'm less kind of bearish going forward, even though I'm not particularly much of a bull either. I think that we will get shorter on the front of the curve. I think the longer end of the curve is probably in a choppy sideways pattern. So it's a tradable asset, but it's not particularly compelling from like a longer-term investing standpoint. You get potentially a lot of volatility without a ton of returns from it.

I'm not that worried about credit quality overall. I think there's obviously some pockets that are concerning, but they tend to -- it's a more slow unfolding default cycle, like, for example, commercial real estate and so forth. Those tend to not be big enough to sync other parts of the economy, unlike, for example, the subprime mortgage crisis was -- it was large enough. It hit right at the core of residential housing. I think, fortunately, we're not in that situation.

So these more kind of smaller pockets where, you might have a multi $100 billion problem, which sounds like a big thing, but when you're talking in the size of the U.S. market, with well over like a 100 trillion in assets out there, those tends -- issues tend to be absorbable.

So I don't focus heavily on the -- there are credit analysts that dive a lot deeper than me, but I mainly look out for big things that could affect macro. And I tend to think that they're -- at the current sizes, they're probably more contained to their areas.

Mike Larson: I guess, last question is kind of odd one because surprises by their nature are surprising. Is there anything that you think could be surprising or anything that might be we haven't talked about already that really is something an investor should be keeping their eye on here?

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Yeah. It's a good question. I think a still big question - I'm not the first one to raise this - is how long is this CapEx cycle in AI going to go. Basically, the GPU cycle, the data centers, and all that. Because if you look at the broad economy, most pockets are kind of stagnant, not doing amazing, and anything kind of tech- or AI-related is kind of lifting everyone up. And so as soon as that slows, that's another pinch of catalyst for global capital to want to get out. Then you have that inverse wealth effect potentially.

And so, whenever you have a very financialized economy where rising stock prices fuel consumption and then that consumption fuels rising stock prices, the problem is that virtuous cycle works well up on the rising side, but then it can kind of surprise you with a vicious cycle on the other side. And well, I don't think we're there yet. It is something that I do monitor pretty closely, just because, again, there's all this capital already stuffed into U.S. markets, and it doesn't take much to at least get a very kind of slight reversal.

Mike Larson: Okay. Well, Lyn, I want to thank you so much for taking some time out here to join the podcast.

Lyn Alden Schwartzer: Thank you.

Originally Published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UVXY: Leveraged ETF Is A Put Option Surrogate For S&P 500 Bears
data.description:
Seriously...would you buy a stock that had a chart like this?

How about if I also showed you the top 10 holdings...and you saw that only 2 are listed, which cover only 10% of the assets? Would you consider this ETF now?

We both know the answer to that pair of questions is NO. Hard pass. No way. Or, to quote tennis legend John McEnroe, "you can't be serious." But I am.

Because I have used a couple of types of ETFs as a surrogate for calls and puts. And I currently own a small postion in UVXY. Why? Because as I see it, the 2 most likely outcomes of owning it (or more accurately trading it) at times like this are:

1. It falls in value, losing a good portion of what I invested, maybe more than half within a matter of months.

2. The S&P 500 flops, quickly. Like it did earlier this year. And in 2022, and 2020. And 2018. And several other times, in bull markets and otherwise.

UVXY is an ETF that is entirely misunderstood, except by those who have experienced what it can do when viewed not in isolation, but alongside an otherwise large cap oriented stock portfolio. Because UVXY is about 1 thing: exploiting volatility during those occasional times that it tanks. That usually happens for reasons that were overdue, but not unexpected. Like now.

None of that is to say I think that the market is ready to implode. But I do think risk is abnormally high. So while I continue to try to "crush it" to the upside with just about every other person in the Seeking Alpha community, UVXY can actually be one of the best alpha-producing ETFs you'll ever find. But only if you know how to use it properly.

UVXY is a leveraged ETF that aims to move 200% in the same direction of the VIX Volatility Index each day. VIX is essentially the S&P 500's "fear gauge." When it is low like it is now (around 15) that implies that the market is in cruise control, without major concerns. That can last a while...or it can quickly turn on a dime. And when it does, UVXY is as potent a weapon to defend the rest of an equity portfolio as I know.

Outside of put options, that is. I write about and coach about options a lot to my Seeking Alpha investing group. It is perhaps the top reason folks join us. We all know how to play offense, in our own preferred way. And we learn from others in this community.

Who's teaching Seeking Alpha subscribers how they can also play defense?

Not many people, from what I can see. I used to take care of families that made a lot of money doing something other than investing in stocks and bonds. So I learned very early in my career that keeping it was more important than taking big risks to make more. Essentially, I learned to be a "defensive coordinator." It worked well, and I retired from that fiduciary role at age 56. In part because I realized that there was a gaping hole in the mass market for educating investors. I wanted to fill it.

Guess what? Other than a few months here and there, this entire decade I've been retired from advising people directly and personally, we have not needed protection from major, sustained stock market declines. Not at all.

But we might in the future. And from my experience using UVXY and some of the other VIX-driven ETFs shown here intermittently over the past several years, I know that it can be an effective surrogate for put options. Like S&P 500 puts, UVXY has those 2 main outcomes. It can lose most or close to all of its value within a year's time. Or, it can rise by several hundred percent quickly. I'll show examples of both in a single chart in a moment.

The reason UVXY is NOT a long-term holding is that the math of loss on leveraged ETFs is already working against investors when the underlying asset is a stock index. But VIX is even more sensitive to this.

I don't normally talk a lot about "position size" here, though it is one of the best risk management techniques I know. Think about it this way: if you own a 10% portfolio position and it drops 10% you lost 1% of your entire portfolio. If you owned a 2% position, it would have to lose half its value to impact your portfolio in dollars by that same amount. Sure, there's less upside.

Ah, but not with UVXY. It is as much a "go for it" ETF as I track. It really is like a put option to me, and as such I tend to buy it in position sizes of perhaps 1-2% of portfolio. That's a bit higher than I would allocate to S&P 500 put options. The puts can expire worthless by a certain date. UXVY doesn't technically expire, but it can lose most of its value over the course of a year. So it needs to be handled with care...or not at all.

That's why I'm attaching this, from the ProShares website page for UVXY:

UVXY's holdings

The holdings for this ETF are not much more than what we saw above. These types of ETFs hold futures. In the case of UVXY, VIX futures out 1 and 2 months, which are rolled as they approach expiration. Cash backs up these futures postions, in a formula that produces that 2X leveraged effect.

UVXY doesn't require options approval on a brokerage account. It is just an ETF, albeit it one that moves wildly. That said, with options you put less money at risk to get similar upside. But you also have a time limit. I've used VIX-based ETFs like VIXY or the 2x UVXY to simulate put options.

UVXY: expected performance

Now, to the bottom line: UVXY makes money when the VIX rises. The more it rises, and especially the faster it rises, the better UVXY does. During times like this current summer period, UVXY's value slowly melts away, akin to how a intermediate-term put option's value on the S&P 500 might.

Here's one of a few performance charts to help understand what UVXY's range of possible outcomes might be, based on the past. That spike in 2020 is not a data error. When the pandemic hit and the S&P 500 fell 34% in just 5 weeks, UVXY gained 800%. I'm happy to say I was aboard in small size for much of that.

This year's path of UVXY is a good summary of the highs and lows to expect here. Up by double when the market dipped 20% quickly, then lost it all and more as the rapid and accelerated recovery ensued. Again, position size is everything here.

UVXY: in my investment trading toolbox

This is one I hold now in small size, and will be happy to increase at the next little sign of serious trouble. I use it interchangably with put options, and in place of them in one account I have that I intentionally did not get options approval on. If handled with a purpose and with humility, it can be a excellent defensive weapon. Just don't try to make it something it is not. Its a tool, not an investment.

Oh, I've sold it at a loss more often than not, but having VIX-driven ETF positions and/or put options on the S&P 500 is a central part of how I handle the "sleep well at night" part of my investment process. I'm very willing to accept a series of small and controllable losses along the way, in order to have something spiking up in value when the stock market is crashing. Even temporarily. Because I think surprise attacks by the equity market is much more likely in this era of algorithms and index trading than in past market cycles.

data.title: Despite The Cautious Guidance, We Upgrade Best Buy To A Buy
data.description:
Best Buy (NYSE:BBY) is a U.S. based firm that sells tech products and services in the United States and internationally. We started covering the company back in June 2022, and published a total of 6 articles since then, always staying neutral on the firm's stock.

As BBY recently reported its quarterly earnings results, and a lot changed in the macroeconomic landscape since over last writing, we think it is time to give an updated view on the firm in general and its valuation. Our aim is to answer questions whether the current valuation could provide an attractive buying opportunity for investors, or if the underperformance compared to the broader market is justified, and if this underperformance is likely to last in the coming months and quarters or not.

Results and macroeconomic landscape

BBY reported its quarterly earnings results recently, reporting a beat on top- and bottomline estimates. Revenue came in at $9.44B, $210M above analyst estimates, representing a year-over-year growth of 1.6%. EPS came in at $1.28 or $0.06 above estimates.

While we believe that it is a good sign that BBY managed to beat analyst estimates despite the tough macroeconomic headwinds, and that they achieved positive YoY sales growth, we do not think that this alone justifies to be bullish on the stock. Why do we think so?

1.) Consumer confidence in the U.S. remains weak. Consumer confidence is a leading economic indicator, which can be used to gauge how the spending behaviour on the consumer may change in the near future.

When sentiment is poor, people are less likely to spend on non-essential, durable, discretionary goods. BBY's products and services fall into these categories. On the earnings call it was pointed out the customers remain cautious about big-ticket purchases. For this reason, we believe that the demand for BBY's products is likely to remain weak for the rest of the year. Management is also not confident that despite the strong quarterly performance they will be able to keep the momentum in the whole year, as a result they left their guidance unchanged, causing the stock price to slide.

"For the full year, Best Buy (BBY) kept its prior outlook for revenue to fall in the range of $41.1 billion to $41.9 billion and a comparable sales range of anywhere from a decline of 1% to an increase of 1%."

2.) Weakening profitability is also a concern. BBY's gross profit margin declined by 10 bps compared to the prior year to 23.4%. The decline was primarily driven by higher sales mix of lower-margin categories. We believe that this could be an indication that due to the weak demand, BBY is not being able to effectively pass on increased costs to its customers.

3.) We also have to mention positive developments. BBY managed to increase its international sales by 11.3%, primarily as a result of Best Buy Express location opened in Canada and a comparable sales growth of 7.6%. We normally believe that having a diversified revenue stream geographically is a positive sign. On the other hand, keep in mind that the revenue generated abroad is impacted by FX fluctuations.

All in all, we believe that despite the revenue and earnings beat, the current macroeconomic environment is not favourable for BBY's business and the consumer discretionary sector in general. Management cautious guidance also supports this thesis.

Valuation

To estimate a range of fair values for BBY's stock, we are going to use dividend discount models, just like we did in our previous writing. Back then, we came up with a range of fair values between $52 and $80, assuming a required return of 9.75% and dividend growth rates between 2.5% and 5% in perpetuity.

As Best Buy remains in the mature growth phase, and keeps paying sustainable dividends, we believe that applying dividend discount models is still a valid approach for valuing the company's stock.

The two parameters that need to be reviewed and updated, if necessary, are the required rate of return and the dividend growth rate.

Required rate of return

For the required rate of return we normally like to use the company's weighted average cost of capital (OTC:WACC), as the company needs to earn at least this return on their projects to recover the cost of their capital and be profitable.

Currently, BBY's WACC is 6.4%, significantly lower than the 9.75% that we used before. This is going to positively impact our fair value estimates.

Dividend growth

BBY managed to keep increasing its dividend throughout the past two decades, consistently. To come up with a reasonable growth estimate, we generally like to look at historic dividend growth rates.

Over the past 10 years, BBY increased its dividend by as much as 16.23% CAGR. While we believe that longer term growth trends are more useful, assuming a double digit dividend growth in perpetuity would lead to a significant overestimation of the fair value.

We are going to calculate two cases:

The first one assumes 0% growth until 2033, assuming that the macroeconomic environment will remain weak. In our view this a relative pessimistic scenario, but could serve us well for a low case estimate. From 2033 onwards, we assume a 2% growth in perpetuity, roughly equivalent to the overall growth of the U.S. economy.

These inputs result in a fair value of $81 per share, roughly 12% above the current share price.

The second one assumes that BBY will keep growing their dividends at the same rate as they did in the previous year - 1.89% - in perpetuity. Using this more optimistic set of inputs, gives us a fair value of $89 per share, representing a 24% upside compared to the current share price.

Based on these calculations, BBY's stock appears to be undervalued. While the macroeconomic landscape is indeed uncertain, we believe that based on the firm's dividends and dividend growth, there could be potential upside for investors buying at the current levels. Bare in mind that due to the poor sentiment and tariff uncertainties near term volatility in the share price is likely.

Conclusions

BBY beat analysts estimates top- and bottomline in the previous quarter, but remained cautious with its full year guidance. We believe that the caution is warranted due to the poor consumer confidence levels and the declining profitability compared to the prior year.

On the other hand, the firm's stock appears to be attractive based on its dividend and dividend growth. Our estimates show a fair value between $81 and $89, representing an upside of 12% to 24%.

For these reasons, we upgrade BBY's stock to a cautious buy.

data.title: BlackRock Health Sciences Opportunities Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of -4.52% (Institutional shares) and -4.58% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s positive relative performance was driven by security selection. At the industry level, biotechnology was the largest contributor to relative returns, while pharmaceuticals detracted.
  • During the quarter, the fund increased its exposure to medical devices, where it added exposure to the health care equipment industry. The allocation to the health care services industry was increased while the exposure to the biotechnology industry was reduced.
Contributors Detractors
An underweight position in UnitedHealth (UNH) was the top contributor to relative returns at a security level. The managed health care company’s stock struggled due to executive reshuffling and lower forward-looking guidance, given expected medical costs. An overweight position in Boston Scientific (BSX) was helpful, as the health care equipment company’s share price rose due to strong financial performance and solid fundamentals. An underweight position in Eli Lilly (LLY) was the top detractor at a security level. The pharmaceuticals company’s stock rose following strong financial performance and continued momentum due to demand for glucagon-like peptide-1 drugs. Not holding Veeva Systems (VEEV) hampered relative returns as the health care technology company’s share price rose following better-than-expected quarterly results.

Further insight

The health care sector struggled during the quarter, as policy rhetoric surrounding tariffs, President Donald Trump’s Most Favoured Nations executive order on prescription drug pricing, and proposed Medicaid cuts continued to overshadow solid fundamentals. We continue to expect a high degree of stock dispersion, driven by increasing scientific innovation, emerging technologies, and policy shifts. All of this underscores a flexible approach to investing in the sector, while emphasizing scientific attributes at the company level. With a more stable earnings profile and valuations trading below long-term averages, we see a favorable risk-reward profile for the sector.

data.title: ACWV Vs. USMV: Should You Get Global Diversification Via Minimum Volatility ETFs?
data.description:
The current economic environment is tense, and the volatility is high due to trade wars, geopolitical tensions and the anticipated rate cut schedule. In this environment, it could prove to be wise to invest in minimum volatility funds. In this article, I will compare two funds, the iShares MSCI Global Min Vol Factor ETF (BATS:ACWV) and the iShares MSCI USA Min Vol Factor ETF (BATS:USMV) to determine if you should focus on the U.S. or have a global scope.

I will rate both funds a buy, but I believe ACWV offers a superior opportunity relative to its U.S. centric peer because emerging economies have proven to deliver higher risk adjusted returns with a low volatility strategy. USMV has outperformed on longer time frames because of its higher weight on tech. If you don’t expect a market shift soon, you could go for USMV or complement ACWV with specific exposure to tech.

Minimum Volatility Investing

The Case for Minimum Volatility

I previously covered the Global X Adaptive U.S. Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:AUSF) here. AUSF is a three factor ETF with a minimum volatility component so I discussed the advantage of minimum volatility extensively for this ETF. If you want details, you can read it.

If you don’t want to bother, here are the key takeaways:

  • It originates from the Modern Portfolio Theory developed by Harry Markowitz in 1952.

  • The S&P 500 minimum volatility index has returned almost the same CAGR since 1998 as the S&P 500 (8.30% vs. 8.33%) but with a standard deviation more than two percentage points below.

Minimum Volatility in the US and Globally

If I believe minimum volatility strategies are a strong option for the defensive part of your portfolio, one important question to ask is where your geographical focus should be. When looking at historical returns (last 10 years), we see that the U.S. ETF USMV delivers higher returns. The easy answer is to say that investing in the U.S. is the thing to do, but I want to add some elements to the discussion.

S&P Global published an excellent article on minimum volatility investing, and while the article is quite old (2019), I believe it is a useful read. The article discusses the “low-volatility anomaly”, which is the fact I highlighted in the previous part, that on a risk adjusted basis, low risk stocks are outperforming. One of the key takeaways from this article is that the outperformance of low volatility is not unique to the U.S., but is a global phenomenon. The article also highlights that minimum volatility is especially strong in weak markets, and less appealing in strong markets. Considering the last few years of abnormal returns for the stock market, you will not be surprised to see in the performance part below that our ETFs are not beating the S&P 500. However, valuations in the market are now really high (dotcom bubble level high), and I think it is time to build defensive positions.

An empirical study published in the Journal of Banking & Finance in 2013 specifically targets the subject of this article, and notes that “low volatility stocks earn higher returns than high volatility stocks in emerging markets and developed markets outside of North America”. So, in 2013, I would have directly stated that ACWV is superior to USMV. My only problem is that this study is 12 years old, and we lack similar up to date studies. The closest study out there is the one of the MSCI R&D team published in May, and including a 25 years of historical data, showing the Relative performance of min-vol Index, splitting emerging markets and the U.S. What comes out is that the relative performance in the U.S. has decreased since 2016, while the relative performance for emerging markets followed the same trend, but with a much stronger recovery. It is also important to note that in both cases the relative performance vs. the classic index (not low volatility) is positive. It is the post covid period that has been difficult to navigate for low volatility strategies, this result is consistent with the S&P Global article of 2019.

In summary, exposure to the U.S. works best when markets are performing best, while a diversified strategy works best in an event of market stress. Considering you are probably considering minimum volatility as a defensive investment, I would recommend the global ETF so far.

Portfolio Analysis

ACWV is tracking the MSCI ACWI Minimum Volatility Index. The fund holds 386 with a 12.28% top 10 concentration and spreads across many countries. Roughly 60% of the fund is invested in the U.S., the rest is 15% in other developed economies and 25% in emerging countries. I like how significant the emerging market exposure is given the above analysis, and the mix of countries allows to capture different economic cycles and decorrelate the returns with the U.S. (even if 60% of the fund remains invested in the U.S.).

USMV on the other hand is only investing in the U.S. The fund is tracking the MSCI USA Minimum Volatility Index, with only 170 holdings and a 13.27% top 10 concentration. USMV is more exposed to risks like trade policy shifts or FED actions.

Both funds, while operating with a different investment universe, have the same strategy of targeting companies with low historical volatility, but this delivers very different risk profiles. Conceptually, because USMV bets on the resiliency of the U.S. market to offer protection, while ACWV bets on international diversification. In practice, we see it with both funds’ sharpe ratios: 0.38 for ACWV and 0.44 for USMV. USMV appears to deliver a higher risk adjusted return, and this is also linked with the difference in sectoral split, not only about geographies. See below:
